questions.text: I find that I wake up at roughly the same emotional baseline every morning regardless of what happened the day before.
I rarely overrule a group decision, even when I think there is a better option.
I tend to present a completed analysis rather than share my work in progress for input.
I often establish priorities for the group without being asked.
I routinely spend my free time on activities that benefit me personally.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral demand that should take precedence over comfort or convenience.
I prepare agendas before any meeting I'm responsible for.
I reciprocate favors when the opportunity naturally arises rather than tracking them.
I can sense when two people are pretending to get along but actually dislike each other.
I think that structured, logical engagement with human problems is a more reliable path to justice than empathic immersion.
I am open to revisiting any decision if the group raises new concerns.
I routinely document my own accomplishments so they are recognized and remembered.
I think through potential problems before they arise so I can prevent them.
I rarely pick up on when a person is signaling discomfort through fidgeting or restlessness.
My body seems to amplify emotional signals — what starts as mild annoyance quickly becomes agitation.
I find that I can hold someone's pain in mind as a fact to be addressed without it becoming a feeling I experience.
I focus entirely on the task at hand and clean up everything at the end in one pass.
I find that the felt weight of another's suffering is the experience that most consistently reveals my true moral commitments.
I find that my body reacts strongly to emotional situations — racing heart, flushed skin, tight chest.
I like to wait until a clear consensus emerges before acting.
I tend to feel emotionally prepared for whatever comes, because my system doesn't overreact.
I am usually more aware of the logical flow of a conversation than its emotional currents.
I feel that building a legacy requires sustained, deliberate effort over many years.
I hold the view that my personal reading list, podcast queue, and study plan should reflect only my interests.
I believe that the moral force of suffering is what makes ethical reasoning necessary rather than optional.
I routinely hand off leads, clients, or referrals to colleagues who need them more.
I notice that I can shift my attention away from emotional stimuli relatively easily.
I find that I am not naturally tuned in to the emotional register of a conversation.
I feel uneasy spending on luxuries for myself when others around me are struggling.
I believe that the ability to examine suffering without flinching — to hold it at a distance and assess it — is essential for meaningful intervention.
I can tell when someone is including unnecessary details in a story to justify themselves.
I hold the view that my education was given to me so I could teach others.
I tend to underestimate how long transitions take and adjust as I go.
When I have a vision, I pursue it whether or not others initially agree.
When something touches me, the feeling doesn't just visit — it moves in and stays.
I pick up on the implicit rules of a social gathering without anyone explaining them to me.
